steps.text: Conduct the sampling device standard check prior to initial use and at least every six months, and after replacement of components, or collection of potentially contaminating samples.
Collect a humidified (> 40% RH) known-standard challenge gas through the sampling device and into a canister.
Analyze the sample according to Section 11 of this method and record the concentration measurement and maintain the results as part of a laboratory record associated with the sampling device.
After establishing the target pressure in the canister, close the valve and attach a vacuum/pressure gauge.
Open the valve and record the initial pressure reading.

Close the valve, remove the vacuum/pressure gauge, and loosely cap the canister using a cap fitting to ensure that leakage through the valve is accurately assessed while avoiding potential entry of debris into the valve during storage.
After a minimum of three days in storage, reinstall the vacuum/pressure gauge, open the valve, and record the canister pressure reading.

Determine the pressure decay rate as the absolute value of the difference between the initial and post-storage canister pressures. You must remove the canister from service if the pressure decay rate exceeds 0.69 kPa/storage day (0.2 in. Hg or 0.1 psia/storage day).
Qualify new canisters before initial use and qualify all canisters used for sampling at least once every 6 months.
Pressurize the clean evacuated canister with humidified (> 50% RH) HCF zero air.
Allow the canister to equilibrate for a minimum of 24 hours.
After the equilibration period, conduct an initial cleanliness analysis as specified in Section 8.4 of this method.
Store the canister for a holding period equal to or exceeding the typical laboratory holding time, nominally 7 days from the canister fill date.
After the holding period, conduct a subsequent cleanliness analysis as specified in Section 8.5 of this method.
The results of both the initial and subsequent cleanliness analysis must meet the cleanliness criteria specified in Section 8.5 of this method to be used for sampling.
You must reclean and retest canisters that fail the zero-air challenge.

Attach the canisters to an oil-free roughing pump and evacuate to approximately 7 kPa absolute (28 in. Hg vacuum or 1.0 psia) with the exhaust of the pump directed to a fume hood or passed through a charcoal trap.
Refill canisters to ambient pressure with HCF zero air.
Attach the canisters to the cleaning system after completing the pre-evacuation and refilling steps.
Place the canister in a location that protects the canister and sampling inlet from unwanted tampering, damage, or theft.
Protect the canister and sampling inlets by placing the canister under shelter, if possible.
Do not restrict air flow around inlets and do not locate inlets under building overhangs.
Do not place the canister near vegetation or structures that block or significantly restrict air flow to the MFCD inlet or manifold.
Ensure that rain cannot be drawn directly into the MFCD, and the inlet heights must be approximately 1.5 to 3 m above ground level.
Determine the appropriate number and placement of sampling locations at the fenceline based on the applicable standard.
Document all activities associated with sampling on the field data page.
Verify that each canister has been cleaned and blanked within the last 30 days.
Label each canister with a sample ID code and record the canister and sample ID on the field data page.
Do not use a canister for sampling that has not been cleaned and blanked within 30 days of sampling.
Verify the sample device is in working order and calibrate/verify the flow rate setting, if applicable, with a reference flow meter.
Record the sample device ID, expected flowrate, and the reference flowrate if calibrated/verified in the field, including the reference flow meter if applicable.
Attach the sampling device to the canister and locate at the appropriate sampling location.
Record the sampling location ID, latitude, longitude, date, and time that you installed the canister on the field data page.
Measure and record the canister vacuum using the field pressure measurement gauge, and verify that the canister has not leaked and has sufficient vacuum to collect the sample.
Replace the canister if the initial pressure is not within -1 in. Hg absolute zero (-3.39 kPa or -0.5 psi).
Conduct leak checks as specified in Section 8.3.2 of this method and record the results on the field data page.

Open the canister valve. Record the date and time that you opened the valve as the start time, and record the initial canister vacuum/pressure and any other comments such as unusual events or conditions that may impact sample results on the field data page.
Sample for the period as defined in the applicable standard (e.g., 24 hours +/- 1 hour).
At the end of the sampling period, close the valve. Record the date and time that you closed the valve as the end time.
Remove the sampling device and attach the field pressure measurement gauge.

Open the canister valve and measure and record the final canister vacuum/pressure and any other comments such as unusual events or conditions that may impact sample results on the field data page. Flag any canisters with a final pressure greater than -3 in. Hg gauge pressure (-10.2 kPag or -1.5 psig).
Disconnect the field canister pressure measurement gauge and replace with a cap.
If applicable, verify the sample device is still in working order and verify the flow rate setting with a reference flow meter. Record the final flowrate on the field data page.
For each sampling day, include the collection of a separate co-located sample for at least one sampling location. The collocated duplicate must be sampled using a discrete MFCD.
For each sample day, collect canister field blanks. A canister blank is prepared by filling a canister with humidified clean diluent gas.
Collect canister field blanks for each sample day.
Prepare a canister with humidified clean diluent gas to approximately -15 in. Hg ± 1 in. Hg.
Record the pressure of the canister blank.
Transport the canister blank to the field site(s) to accompany field-collected canisters.
Treat canister field blanks identically to field-collected samples in the field and laboratory.
Analyze the field blanks by interspersing them among the field samples.
Collect a canister field spike for each sample day.
Prepare a canister with humidified standard gas at a concentration in the lower third of the calibration curve to approximately -15 in. Hg ± 1 in. Hg.
Transport the field spike canister to the field site to accompany field-collected canisters.
Treat field spikes identically to field-collected samples in the field and laboratory.
Test the port of the autosampler to demonstrate cleanliness by analyzing humidified zero air.
Heat and purge any lines.
Rinse with deionized water, dry, and purge any lines that fail.
Test each port of the autosampler with a reference standard to demonstrate that the autosampler is not causing bias.
Heat and purge any lines.
Rinse with deionized water, dry, and purge any lines that fail.
Perform a pressure decay test of a canister pressurized to approximately 203 kPa absolute over the course of several days.
Remove the canister from service and repair canister connections and/or valve.
Fill canisters with humidified zero air to determine that they are acceptably clean over the course of 7 days.
Clean and retest canisters.
Open the canister valves.
Document the leak check results in the analysis records.
Connect each sample canister to the preconcentration unit through a port.
Verify each canister as having a leak-free connection.
Accurately measure the sample aliquot volume for analysis by metering the sample.
Begin sample analysis.

preconditions.text: If the unit cannot be cleaned to meet the specifications, retire the unit from use or repurpose for source sampling.
You must not use a canister for sampling that has not been cleaned and blanked within 30 days of sampling.
Prior to initial use, upon replacement of transfer lines, or after analysis of potentially contaminating samples.
Prior to initial use and upon replacement of transfer lines.
Prior to initial use and annually thereafter.
Initially upon receipt in the laboratory and every 6 months thereafter.
The compound cannot be positively identified if any of the four criteria are not met.

constraints.text: Each target VOC’s concentration must be < 20 pptv or preferably not detected.
Each target VOC’s concentration within ± 15% of theoretical concentration.
Each target VOC’s concentration ≤ 20 pptv at 101.3 kPa absolute.
